curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Interpret and construct distance–time graphs, linking gradient to speed and identifying stops, changes of speed and total distance travelled from a journey graph.

verse_ality.driving_question: How can a distance–time graph tell the story of a journey — including speed changes and stops?

knowing_world.focus: Construct and interpret a distance–time graph for a journey, using the slope of each segment to describe speed and identifying intervals of constant speed, acceleration-like change (via changing gradients), and rest (flat sections).
key_concepts: Total distance travelled vs position from start
tools: Interactive: Journey Builder & Distance-Time Graph Web App (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG5UQ0Y9nk/Qjl71KGEfDbuykOgH_36IQ/view?embed

human_story.focus: Connect graphs to everyday movement: routines, commutes, deliveries, and travel patterns, and consider how different people experience journeys differently.
justice_prompts: Imagine this journey is a daily routine. What real-world contexts could this graph represent?
Who might experience such movement (and what might make the journey easier or harder for them)?

expressive_self.focus: Turn a graph into a narrative by matching segments to events and emotions, using time and movement creatively to tell a coherent story.
activities: Create a story that matches your graph (what happens during each segment?).
Use dramatic moments: sudden stops, rushing, steady travel, delays.
Describe how the pace of movement could reflect mood or energy.

designed_world.focus: Use distance–time graphs as a design tool for analysing motion in systems like transport and robotics, where efficiency, timing and planning depend on reading and communicating movement accurately.
design_challenges: Design a journey that minimises travel time without increasing maximum speed too much.
Design a robot delivery route with planned stops and speed changes, then represent it on a distance–time graph.
Explain how a transport engineer could use your graph to improve efficiency or scheduling.

living_body.focus: Link graph features to bodily effort: walking, running, resting, and changes in energy, and notice how different paces feel across time.
regulation_supports: Notice the difference between a walking pace and a running pace and how each would look on a distance–time graph.
Add a rest interval to your journey and describe what your body would need during that time.

pathways.name: Final Task: Design and Explain a Journey Graph
pathways.focus: Design your own distance–time graph journey with stops and speed changes, then explain what the graph shows using correct graph language (including slope as speed).
products: A completed journey graph (screenshot or exported image from the interactive).
A written, audio, or video explanation describing each interval (moving/stopped, faster/slower).
At least three questions answered about your graph (e.g., fastest segment, total time, total distance, where you stopped).
Optional: peer review or comparison with a classmate’s journey.

required: Read scales on axes and interpret coordinates
Understand time intervals and distance units
Basic idea of gradient/steepness as a rate of change (informal is fine)

builds_toward: Interpreting real-world graphs and rates of change
Linear graphs and gradient in algebra
